40522761.title: Potential common pathogenesis of several neurodegenerative diseases.
40522761.abstract: With the gradual advancement of research methods and technologies, various biological processes have been identified as playing roles in the pathogenesis of neurodegenerative diseases. However, current descriptions of these biological processes do not fully explain the onset, progression, and development of these conditions. Therefore, exploration of the pathogenesis of neurodegenerative diseases remains a valuable area of research. This review summarizes the potential common pathogeneses of Alz
40522761.authors: Fan T, Peng J, Liang H, Chen W, Wang J et al. (6 authors)

analysis.finding: Research findings have indicated that several common biological processes contribute to the pathogenesis of neurodegenerative diseases.

40650880.title: Brain atrophy patterns in anti-IgLON5 disease.
40650880.abstract: Anti-IgLON5 disease is an autoimmune encephalitis that presents with a heterogenous clinical phenotype, including sleep disorders, movement abnormalities and bulbar involvement. It is characterized by autoantibodies against IgLON5, 85% association with HLA-DQB1*05:∼ and a brainstem-dominant tauopathy. Cellular and murine models report pathogenic effects of the autoantibodies, and neurodegenerative factors suggest progressive atrophy as a common sequela. However, evidence from in vivo patient dat
40650880.authors: Yogeshwar SM, Bartels F, Grüter T, Muñiz-Castrillo S, Picard G et al. (39 authors)

analysis.finding: Brain atrophy patterns in anti-IgLON5 disease show a brainstem-dominant tauopathy.

41813136.title: ALS and Huntington Disease: Unraveling the Connections between TDP-43 and Huntingtin.
41813136.abstract: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and Huntington disease (HD) are lethal neurodegenerative diseases affecting motor function. Though their etiology and pathology are distinct, recent evidence suggests commonalities between TAR DNA-binding protein (TDP-43), which is associated with 97% of ALS cases, and huntingtin (HTT), the causative protein of HD. ALS is a heterogeneous, lethal neurodegenerative disease characterized by the progressive loss of upper and lower motor neurons, as well as brainst
41813136.journal: The Journal of neuroscience : the official journal of the Society for Neuroscience

analysis.finding: Recent evidence suggests commonalities between TDP-43 and huntingtin in the pathology of ALS and HD.
analysis.novel_insight: The study highlights a potential link between two distinct neurodegenerative diseases, suggesting a shared molecular mechanism.

40576748.title: Innovative Interventions: Postbiotics and Psychobiotics in Neurodegenerative Disease Treatment.
40576748.abstract: Neurodegenerative disorders, including Huntington's disease, Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, Alzheimer's disease, and Parkinson's disease, create more challenges as the population gets older and there are no curative therapies available. Recent advances in gut microbiome research have spotlighted postbiotics and psychobiotics as innovative therapeutic strategies targeting the gut-brain axis to alleviate neurodegenerative symptoms and slow disease progression. Postbiotics, which are metabolites an
40576748.authors: Gupta MK, Chauhan K, Bhardwaj S, Srivastava R

analysis.finding: Postbiotics and psychobiotics may offer novel approaches to neuroprotection in neurodegenerative diseases.

41295902.title: Huntington's disease clinical trials update: October 2025.
41295902.abstract: In this edition of the Huntington's Disease Clinical Trials Update, we expand on the launch of the phase II/III clinical trial of SKY-0515 from Skyhawk Therapeutics and the phase I/II clinical trial of SPK-10001 from Spark Therapeutics. We also report positive topline data from uniQure's phase I/II clinical trial of AMT-130 after 36 months of follow-up. Further updates include recent developments in Roche's tominersen programme within GENERATION HD2, progress with votoplam (PTC518) in PIVOT-HD b
41295902.authors: Farag M, Tabrizi SJ, Wild EJ

analysis.finding: Several clinical trials for Huntington's disease show promising results, including positive topline data from uniQure's phase I/II trial of AMT-130.

41841355.title: Discovery and Optimization of Thienopyrazine RNA-Splicing Modulators for the Treatment of Huntington's Disease.
41841355.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder caused by a CAG-repeat expansion in the Huntington gene (HTT). Herein, we describe the discovery of a series of HTT pre-mRNA-splicing modulators that promote the inclusion of a cryptic stop codon that in turn lowers levels of mutant Huntington protein (mHTT). Optimization of the starting thienopyridine amide core resulted in the discovery of the potent, CNS-penetrant, selective, and orally bioavailable HTT-splicing modulator B
41841355.authors: Xu C, Abraham N, Bansal N, Bolduc PN, Cullen P et al. (31 authors)

analysis.finding: Discovery of a potent, CNS-penetrant HTT-splicing modulator that lowers levels of mutant Huntington protein.
analysis.novel_insight: Optimization of the thienopyridine amide core led to the discovery of a structurally distinct and orally bioavailable HTT-splicing modulator.

41318997.title: The impact of tau deletion on Huntington's disease: An in vivo perspective.
41318997.abstract: Although an emerging body of evidence suggests that abnormal forms of tau are present and contribute to Huntington's disease (HD)-a genetic neurodegenerative disorder primarily characterized by the aggregation of the mutant huntingtin (mHtt) protein affecting cognitive, motor, and psychiatric function-it is not clear to what extent this is relevant to the disease phenotype, and hence future treatments. We therefore generated a novel murine model by crossing heterozygous zQ175 knockin HD mice wit
41318997.journal: Molecular therapy : the journal of the American Society of Gene Therapy
41318997.authors: Lepinay E, Saint-Pierre M, Teixeira M, Snapyan M, Oueslati A et al. (8 authors)

analysis.finding: Tau deletion exacerbated both motor and cognitive deficits in HD mice.
analysis.novel_insight: Tau's role in HD is more significant than previously thought.

41846052.title: Human iPSC-derived exosomes for amelioration of Huntington's disease through mitochondrial, synaptic, and anti-apoptotic mechanisms.
41846052.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is driven by expanded CAG repeats in the huntingtin gene, resulting in mutant huntingtin (mHtt) aggregation, mitochondrial dysfunction, neuronal loss, and neuroinflammation. Although stem cell-based therapies provide potent regenerative, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective effects, their clinical translation remains constrained by tumorigenicity and poor engraftment efficiency. Therefore, we investigated whether exosomes derived from human induced pluripotent stem ce
41846052.authors: Jahng S, Palomar-Alonso N, Moon J, Yang BG, Lee M et al. (6 authors)

analysis.finding: Human iPSC-derived exosomes show potential for ameliorating Huntington's disease through mitochondrial, synaptic, and anti-apoptotic mechanisms.
analysis.novel_insight: Exosomes derived from human induced pluripotent stem cells can serve as a safe, cell-free therapeutic approach for HD.

41238102.title: Targeting lipid metabolism in neurodegenerative diseases: From experimental to clinical.
41238102.abstract: The human brain, despite accounting for only 2 % of total body weight, exhibits an exceptionally high lipid content (approximately 20 % of its mass), highlighting the critical role of lipid metabolism in maintaining neural homeostasis and function. Neurodegenerative diseases-including Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD), stroke, Huntington's disease (HD), and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)-are characterized by progressive neuronal dysfunction and myelin degeneration. These co
41238102.authors: Long J, Liu S, Shi Y, Zhang C, Qin L et al. (6 authors)

analysis.finding: Dysregulated lipid metabolism is involved in the pathogenesis of neurodegenerative diseases.

41721094.title: Gene therapy for huntington's disease: advances, challenges, and future perspectives.
41721094.authors: Kaushal R, Yadav M, Kosey S, Dwivedi M

analysis.finding: Gene therapy approaches show promise in reducing mutant huntingtin protein levels and alleviating disease symptoms.

41827217.title: The New Wave of Gene and Cell Therapies Across Diseases.
41827217.abstract: Recent years have seen rapid progress in biological treatments for genetic diseases, as well as conditions like type 1 diabetes that lack an obvious genetic component. The authors sought to explain why this progress has emerged at this particular moment. The best way to illustrate this is by showcasing a wide range of therapies targeting diverse diseases. This progress has been driven by technological advances in genetically modified CAR-T and CAR-NK cells (e.g., using CRISPR or transgenes), whi
41827217.authors: Rieske A, Grot D, Tręda C, Włodarczyk A, Stoczyńska-Fidelus E et al. (7 authors)

analysis.finding: Recent years have seen rapid progress in biological treatments for genetic diseases.

41783572.title: Copper Homeostasis and Cuproptosis in Neurological Disorders.
41783572.abstract: Neurological disorders such as Alzheimer's disease (AD) and Parkinson's disease (PD) pose a serious global public health threat, with complex etiologies involving genetic, environmental, and metabolic factors. Current data indicate that the prevalence of these disorders is rapidly increasing with the aging population, resulting in a growing economic and healthcare burden worldwide. In recent years, the imbalance of copper homeostasis has been increasingly implicated in the pathogenesis of neurol
41783572.authors: Liu W, Xue Y, Cao C, Yang L, Zhang L

analysis.finding: Copper imbalance contributes to the pathogenesis of neurological diseases by inducing oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction.

40421472.title: Use of antidepressants in early Huntington's Disease: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
40421472.abstract: While neuropsychiatric symptoms are common in Huntington's Disease (HD), there is a dearth of evidence about the effectiveness of psychotropic medication for treating behavioral and cognitive symptoms. This article systematically reviews and aggregates the evidence of the effects of antidepressants on individuals with early HD. A systematic review and random-effects meta-analysis of RCTs comparing antidepressants to placebo in individuals with HD was performed, with a focus on outcomes of execut
40421472.authors: Tiwari V, Dutta S, Alkharboush F, Velit MR, Espinel Z

analysis.finding: A modest but statistically significant improvement in symptoms was observed with antidepressant use in early Huntington's Disease.

41690900.title: Huntington's disease is the best investment in neuroscience today.
41690900.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is usually described as rare, tragic, and intractable. Yet, HD offers a strategically unique entry point for neuroscience. With its genetic clarity, relatively predictable course, and organized global community, HD provides the clearest path to advancing brain repair.

analysis.finding: HD offers a strategically unique entry point for neuroscience due to its genetic clarity and relatively predictable course.

41663779.title: Tailoring treatments: pharmacogenomics in the management of neurodegenerative diseases.
41663779.abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, Huntington's, and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis are growing more common worldwide, yet treatment is still poor. Conventional therapies can have unforeseen side effects, produce poor medication reactions, and take longer to work. This persistent treatment gap highlights the need for novel approaches to these disorders' complex distinctions. Pharmacogenomics, which examines how genetic differences affect drug response, is a promising new su
41663779.authors: Roy KK, Kumari R, Upadhyay AK, Mohanty S

analysis.finding: Pharmacogenomics tailors medicine selection and administration to each patient's genetic profile.

41376158.title: Administration of a barcoded AAV capsid library to the putamen of non-human primates identifies variants with efficient retrograde transport.
41376158.abstract: Adeno-associated viral vectors have become a leading choice for gene therapy in the central nervous system due to their safety profile, efficient neuronal transduction, and capacity for sustained transgene expression. We previously reported that AAV2-derived capsids developed using the BRAVE (Barcoded Rational AAV Vector Evolution) approach have enhanced retrograde transport properties in the rodent brain, compared with parental AAV2. Retrograde transport enables broader coverage of connected br
41376158.journal: Molecular therapy : the journal of the American Society of Gene Therapy
41376158.authors: Dzhashiashvili Y, McBride JL, Fabyanic E, Huang X, Kelly BM et al. (16 authors)

analysis.finding: A barcoded AAV capsid library was used to identify variants with efficient retrograde transport in non-human primates.
analysis.novel_insight: Efficient retrograde transport of AAV vectors can be achieved using a barcoded AAV capsid library, which may improve gene therapy outcomes for Huntington's disease and other neurodegenerative disorders.

41770933.title: Huntington's disease LIG1 modifier variant increases ligase fidelity and suppresses somatic CAG repeat expansion.
41770933.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is a fatal neurodegenerative disorder caused by inheriting an expanded CAG repeat tract in the huntingtin gene (
41770933.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America
41770933.authors: Lee E, Kim W, Beier DH, Lee Y, Kovalenko M et al. (23 authors)

analysis.finding: A modifier variant in the LIG1 gene increases ligase fidelity and suppresses somatic CAG repeat expansion.
analysis.novel_insight: The study reveals a new mechanism by which a genetic variant can influence the progression of Huntington's disease.

41622913.title: The HTT1a protein initiates HTT aggregation in a knock-in mouse model of Huntington's disease.
41622913.abstract: The mutation that causes Huntington's disease is a CAG repeat expansion in exon 1 of the huntingtin gene (HTT) that leads to an abnormally long polyglutamine tract in the huntingtin protein (HTT). Mutant CAG repeats are unstable and increase in size in specific neurons and brain regions with age, a phenomenon that constitutes the first step in the pathogenesis of the disease. In the presence of an expanded CAG repeat, cryptic polyA sites in intron 1 of the HTT pre-mRNA can become activated leadi
41622913.authors: Papadopoulou AS, Landles C, Smith EJ, Bondulich MK, Boeddrich A et al. (19 authors)

analysis.finding: The production of HTT1a protein may be the mechanism through which expanded CAG repeats lead to Huntington's disease.
analysis.novel_insight: HTT1a is a highly aggregation-prone and pathogenic protein generated from cryptic polyA sites in intron 1 of the HTT pre-mRNA.

41755664.title: Contribution of neuroepigenetics to HD - developmental and aging-related signatures.
41755664.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is a neurodegenerative disorder triggered by an unstable expansion of CAG repeats in the coding sequence of the
41755664.authors: Scuto J, Penaud N, Merienne K

analysis.finding: Huntington's disease is triggered by an unstable expansion of CAG repeats in the coding sequence of the gene.

41841481.title: Challenges facing genetic diagnostics of Huntington's disease: an update.
41841481.abstract: Since 1993, when a polymorphic CAG trinucleotide repeat was established as the cause of Huntington's disease (HD), fragment analysis has been the first-tier test in genetic diagnosis of HD. However, in recent years it has become increasingly clear that, in addition to the repeat length itself, sequence variations within the  Sequence interruptions or non-canonical repeat motifs have historically been difficult to identify, as PCR cannot distinguish an interrupted repeat tract when assessed by ca
41841481.authors: Arning L, Saft C, Nguyen HP

analysis.finding: Current genetic diagnostic methods for Huntington's disease may underestimate repeat length due to sequence variations.

41849583.title: Lowering the
41849583.authors: Papadopoulou AS, Alterman J, Landles C, Smith EJ, Conroy F et al. (21 authors)

analysis.finding: Reduced mutant huntingtin protein levels in patient-derived cells.

41849580.title: Selective targeting of mutant
41849580.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) arises from the toxic gain of function caused by a CAG expansion in the coding region of the
41849580.authors: Bragg RM, Landles C, Smith EJ, Osborne GF, Mathews EW et al. (8 authors)

analysis.finding: Huntington's disease arises from the toxic gain of function caused by a CAG expansion in the coding region of the gene.

41738896.title: Zebrafish as a Versatile Screening Model for Neurological Diseases: Insights Into Biology, Drug Delivery and Therapeutic Discovery.
41738896.abstract: Neurological diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, anxiety, depression, and Huntington's disease pose significant challenges due to their complex pathophysiology and limited treatment options. Traditional mammalian models, although widely used, often face limitations in cost, ethical concerns, and scalability. Zebrafish have become an excellent animal model for neurological research in recent years because of their remarkable genetic similarity to humans, well-preserved neur
41738896.journal: Journal of biochemical and molecular toxicology
41738896.authors: Vyavahare S, Pawar A, Sayyed SA, Shegar N, Raykar S et al. (7 authors)

analysis.finding: Zebrafish can be used as a versatile screening model for neurological diseases due to their genetic similarity to humans and well-preserved neurotransmitter systems.

41879495.title: Emerging Roles of Small-molecule Derivatives in Modulating Neurodegenerative Pathways: From Molecular Targets to Therapeutic Applications.
41879495.abstract: Neurodegenerative disorders such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and Huntington's disease involve progressive neuronal dysfunction driven by mechanisms including protein aggregation, oxidative stress, mitochondrial impairment, and neuroinflammation. Current treatments are largely symptomatic, with limited disease-modifying options. Advances in medicinal chemistry have led to the development of small-molecule derivatives targeting specific pathological
41879495.authors: Singh K, Sethi P, Jain D, Gupta JK, Alsaidan OA et al. (9 authors)

analysis.finding: Recent progress in medicinal chemistry has led to the development of small-molecule derivatives targeting specific pathological pathways.

41639620.title: Design and evaluation of semantically-valid negative samples integration techniques for scalable semi-automated drug repurposing prediction pipelines in rare disease research.
41639620.abstract: Computational approaches involving complex data structures (e.g. machine learning, knowledge graphs) have been more prominent in biological studies for the last two decades. Due to increasingly larger amounts of data collected with modern omics techniques, there is a need for methods that can process such data quickly and thoroughly. In addition, those techniques can be applied to extrapolate results from a limited number of observations. Rare disease research benefits particularly from those ne
41639620.authors: Bianchi N, Lefebvre AEJL, Wolstencroft KJ, Spruit M

analysis.finding: Computational approaches can be applied to rare disease research for scalable semi-automated drug repurposing prediction pipelines.

41492395.title: Neural and biomarker correlates of the Parkinson's Disease-Cognitive Rating Scale in Huntington's disease.
41492395.abstract: Cognitive decline is a core feature of Huntington's disease (HD), often preceding motor symptoms and progressing with disease severity. While several neuropsychological tests track cognitive changes, few studies have examined the biological correlates of brief screening tools adapted for HD. This study investigates the neuroanatomical and fluid biomarker correlates of performance on the Parkinson's Disease-Cognitive Rating Scale (PD-CRS), aiming to validate it as a clinically and biologically gr
41492395.authors: Martinez-Horta S, Puig-Davi A, Sampedro F, Pérez-Pérez J, Franch-Martí C et al. (12 authors)

analysis.finding: Plasma neurofilament light chain levels correlate with cognitive decline in Huntington's disease.

41699789.title: Huntington's Disease: An Overview of Biomarkers in Prognostic, Diagnostic, and Therapeutic Management.
41699789.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by motor dysfunction, cognitive decline, and psychiatric disturbances. It is caused by the abnormal expansion of cytosine-adenine-guanine triplet repeats in the HTT gene, leading to the production of mutant huntingtin (mHTT) protein. This protein contributes to neuronal dysfunction and cell death, particularly in the striatum and cortex. The review examines recent research focused on identifying and assessing pot
41699789.authors: Singh G, Singh C, Singh A

analysis.finding: Neurofilament light chain and mHTT emerge as robust indicators of disease progression in Huntington's disease.

41527739.title: Insights into phosphoproteomic studies and prospects of phosphoproteins as biomarkers for brain disorders.
41527739.abstract: The dysregulation of phosphorylation networks plays a critical role in the pathogenesis of a wide spectrum of brain disorders, including Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, traumatic brain injury, ischemic stroke, drug abuse, major depressive disorder, and schizophrenia. Notably, phosphorylated isoforms of microtubule-associated protein tau and neurofilament heavy polypeptide are already utilized in clinical diagnostics, highlighting the
41527739.authors: Le J, Hu X, Jiang Y, Wang Q, Ma Q et al. (6 authors)

analysis.finding: Phosphoproteomic studies reveal potential biomarkers for brain disorders, including Huntington's disease.

41828602.title: A Two-Track Model of Huntington's Disease Pathology: Striatal Atrophy Mediates Maladaptive Immune Dysregulation.
41828602.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is characterized by progressive striatal atrophy and complex proteomic changes in the central nervous system. Using the ultrasensitive Next-Gen Ultra-Sensitive Immunoassay (NULISA) proteomic platform, we analyzed cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) from 88 persons with HD to dissect the biological correlates of gray matter loss. Our findings reveal a distinct "Two-Track" model of pathology. The first track, marked by the axonal damage protein neurofilament light chain (NEFL), sho
41828602.authors: Bockholt HJ, Clemsen JD, Baker BT, Calhoun VD, Paulsen JS

analysis.finding: A distinct 'Two-Track' model of Huntington's disease pathology was identified, with axonal damage protein NEFL inversely correlated with putamen volume.
analysis.novel_insight: The study provides new insights into the relationship between striatal atrophy and immune dysregulation in HD.

41762390.title: Multiple System Atrophy Combined Outcome Assessment (MuSyCA): process, format, and validation plan.
41762390.abstract: The Unified Multiple System Atrophy Rating Scale (UMSARS) is widely used as an outcome measure in MSA trials, but it has limitations for clinical trial use. To address these, we developed the Multiple System Atrophy Combined Outcome Assessment (MuSyCA), a comprehensive multimodal tool for disease-modifying MSA trials. The purpose of this manuscript is to describe the development and validation plan for MuSyCA, with emphasis on its structure, intended use, and assessment of reliability, validity,
41762390.journal: Clinical autonomic research : official journal of the Clinical Autonomic Research Society
41762390.authors: Kaufmann H, Palma JA, Millar Vernetti P, Kuijpers M, Nkrumah G et al. (25 authors)

analysis.finding: A new outcome assessment tool, MuSyCA, was developed to track disease progression in Multiple System Atrophy trials.

41406849.title: Molecular diagnostics in clinical microbiology: Advances, applications, and future directions.
41406849.abstract: Molecular diagnostics have transformed clinical microbiology by enabling rapid, accurate, and highly sensitive detection of infectious agents, significantly improving patient outcomes and public health response. Over the past decade, advances in polymerase chain reaction (PCR), next-generation sequencing (NGS), digital PCR, isothermal amplification, and CRISPR-based assays have enhanced pathogen identification, antimicrobial resistance profiling, and outbreak investigation. These technologies ha
41406849.authors: Asokan S, Choudekar A, Jagadeesan A, Sm R, Ali A et al. (14 authors)

analysis.finding: Molecular diagnostics have transformed clinical microbiology by enabling rapid, accurate, and highly sensitive detection of infectious agents.

41674784.title: CRISPR-Cas technologies in neurodegenerative disorders: mechanistic insights, therapeutic potential, and translational challenges.
41674784.abstract: CRISPR-Cas genome-editing technologies have emerged as powerful tools for precise DNA and RNA modulation, offering promising therapeutic strategies for neurodegenerative disorders such as Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD), Huntington's disease (HD), and amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). This review critically evaluates current CRISPR/Cas applications in neurodegeneration, with emphasis on mechanistic insights, therapeutic outcomes, and translational feasibility. Preclinical a
41674784.authors: Yashooa RK, Nabi AQ, Smail SW, Azeez SS, Nooh WA et al. (9 authors)

analysis.finding: CRISPR-Cas genome-editing technologies show promise in treating neurodegenerative disorders by correcting pathogenic mutations and suppressing toxic gene expression.

41840824.title: CRISPR Gene Editing for Nucleotide Repeat Expansion Disorders: A Systematic Review of Preclinical and Clinical Evidence.
41840824.abstract: Incurable hereditary diseases such as Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD), Huntington's disease (HD), and myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1) fall into the nucleotide repeat expansion disorder (NRED) category. The discovery of CRISPR-Cas genome editing has paved the way toward hopeful strategies for accurate DNA-level repair. This systematic review presents preclinical data on the efficacy, molecular effects, and limitations of CRISPR-based treatments for NREDs. As per Preferred Reporting Items for Sy
41840824.authors: Thalib HI, Khan S, Hanin Shaikh A, Alawi KM, Alabdrabalrasol ZH et al. (9 authors)

analysis.finding: CRISPR-Cas genome editing shows promise for treating nucleotide repeat expansion disorders.

41784089.title: Novel monoclonal antibodies against the C-terminal HEAT domain of huntingtin.
41784089.abstract: BackgroundReliable detection of huntingtin (HTT) is essential for understanding Huntington's disease (HD) biology and for evaluating therapeutic strategies. However, high-quality monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) against the HTT C-terminal domain remain limited.ObjectiveWe sought to generate and validate novel monoclonal antibodies targeting the HTT C-terminal HEAT-containing domain to better detect HTT independently of potential effects of polyglutamine length that can impact some N-terminally targe
41784089.authors: Park YN, Fanti R, Sadeghi S, Chandrasekaran R, Edwards AM et al. (7 authors)

analysis.finding: Novel monoclonal antibodies targeting the HTT C-terminal HEAT-containing domain have been generated and validated.
analysis.novel_insight: Development of high-quality monoclonal antibodies against the HTT C-terminal domain, which can detect HTT independently of polyglutamine length.

41708002.title: The Hsp40 cochaperone DNAJC7 regulates polyglutamine aggregation and exhibits context-dependent effects on polyglycine aggregation.
41708002.abstract: Protein-encoding nucleotide repeat expansion diseases, including polyglutamine (polyQ) and polyglycine (polyG) diseases, are characterized by the accumulation of aggregation-prone proteins. In the polyQ diseases, including Huntington's disease and several spinocerebellar ataxias, substantial prior evidence supports a pathogenic role for mutant polyQ-expanded protein misfolding and aggregation, with molecular chaperones showing promise in suppressing disease phenotypes in cellular and animal mode
41708002.authors: Ramani B, Ehsani K, Kampmann M

analysis.finding: The Hsp40 cochaperone DNAJC7 regulates polyglutamine aggregation and exhibits context-dependent effects on polyglycine aggregation.
analysis.novel_insight: DNAJC7 has a regulatory effect on protein aggregation in Huntington's disease

41849610.title: Self-inactivating AAV-CRISPR at different ages enables sustained amelioration of Huntington's disease deficits in BAC226Q mice.
41849610.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is a monogenic autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder caused by a CAG repeat expansion in exon 1 of the
41849610.authors: Dai Y, Abudujielili Z, Ding Y, Huang W, Yin J et al. (9 authors)

targets: CAG repeat expansion in exon 1 of the
analysis.finding: Sustained amelioration of Huntington's disease deficits was achieved using self-inactivating AAV-CRISPR at different ages in BAC226Q mice.
analysis.novel_insight: Gene therapy approach shows promise for treating HD

41618467.title: Emerging roles of circular RNAs and enhancer RNAs: new insights into the development and management of neurodegenerative disorders.
41618467.abstract: Neurodegenerative disorders involve the gradual breakdown of neurons, leading to problems with thinking, movement, and mental health. More evidence is emerging about the important roles of non-coding RNAs (ncRNAs). CircRNAs are a type of ncRNA formed through back-splicing and are widely present in the mammalian brain. They play crucial roles in brain development and mainly regulate gene expression and post-transcriptional processes by acting as molecular sponges for miRNAs and RBPs. eRNAs are an
41618467.authors: Ziaei A, Kargar M, Shirvani-Farsani Z, MehrabMohseni M

analysis.finding: Abnormal levels of circRNAs and eRNAs can be found in many human diseases, including neurodegenerative conditions.
analysis.novel_insight: The study highlights the emerging roles of non-coding RNAs (ncRNAs) in neurodegenerative disorders.

41833626.title: Autophagy-exosome crosstalk in neurodegeneration: Mechanisms and therapeutic opportunities.
41833626.abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases (NDs), including Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, Huntington's, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and multiple sclerosis, share a common pathogenic signature: disrupted proteostasis driven by impaired autophagy and maladaptive exosome dynamics. Under normal conditions, autophagy maintains neuronal homeostasis by clearing misfolded proteins and damaged organelles, while exosomes mediate neuroglial communication. When autophagic flux is impaired or lysosomal function is compromise
41833626.authors: Sedighi S, Guan T, Michetti F, Cordani M, Barzegar Behrooz A et al. (10 authors)

analysis.finding: Impaired autophagic flux and lysosomal dysfunction can lead to the secretion of pathogenic proteins through exosomes.
analysis.novel_insight: The study highlights a novel mechanism by which neurodegenerative diseases, including Huntington's, may be driven by disrupted autophagy and maladaptive exosome dynamics.

41407165.title: Nutraceutical interventions for neuroprotection: a comprehensive review.
41407165.abstract: Nutraceuticals, bioactive compounds derived from food sources, are emerging as promising agents for neuroprotection, particularly through their modulation of gut health. Unlike conventional single-molecule therapeutics that often target isolated pathways, nutraceuticals offer a multi-targeted approach by influencing the gut-brain axis, a bidirectional communication network linking the gut microbiota and the central nervous system. Key nutraceuticals such as probiotics, prebiotics, polyphenols, o
41407165.authors: Ghosh N, Ghosh J, Ghosh S, Sinha K, Sil PC

analysis.finding: Nutraceuticals offer a multi-targeted approach to neuroprotection by influencing the gut-brain axis.

41353868.title: S-acylation and neuroinflammation: the therapeutic potential of zDHHC and deacylase modulation.
41353868.abstract: Neuroinflammation is a hallmark of many neurodegenerative diseases, including Alzheimer's, Parkinson's and Huntington's disease, multiple sclerosis, and infantile neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis. Dynamic protein S-acylation, a reversible lipid post-translational modification, is an important regulator in these processes. S-acylation is catalysed by the zDHHC palmitoyl acyltransferases, and removal of the acyl groups is mediated by acyl-protein thioesterases. S-acylation controls the localisation,
41353868.authors: Duarte TA, Ng CP, Salvador JAR, Pipito L, Greaves J et al. (6 authors)

analysis.finding: Dysregulated S-acylation contributes to synaptic loss, aberrant immune signalling, and neurodegeneration.

41689141.title: Mitochondrial DNA: a molecular switch driving sterile neuroinflammation.
41689141.abstract: Mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) plays a pivotal role in the regulation of neuroinflammation, acting as a potent trigger of innate immune responses when released into the cytoplasm or extracellular space. mtDNA is structurally similar to bacterial DNA, containing unmethylated CpG motifs that are readily recognized by immune sensors. Under conditions of cellular stress, injury, or mitochondrial dysfunction, mtDNA can escape into the cytoplasm, where it activates the cGAS (cyclic GMP-AMP synthase)-STING
41689141.authors: Jauhari A, Singh T, Carlisle DL, Friedlander RM

analysis.finding: Mitochondrial DNA can trigger sterile neuroinflammation by activating the cGAS-STING signaling pathway or being detected extracellularly by Toll-like receptors.
analysis.novel_insight: mtDNA's role in triggering sterile neuroinflammation is a novel insight

41699331.title: Non-canonical cell death in neurodegeneration: emerging mechanisms and therapeutic Frontiers.
41699331.abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases, specifically Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD), Huntington's disease (HD), and Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) are defined by progressively increased neuronal loss that lacks curative therapies. Increasing evidence supports that non-canonical regulated cell death pathways including ferroptosis, necroptosis, pyroptosis, and parthanatos, are implicated in pathological mechanisms of neuroinflammation, and oxidative stress, and mitochondrial dysfuncti
41699331.journal: Apoptosis : an international journal on programmed cell death
41699331.authors: Ercin N, Besli N, Beker M, Celik U

analysis.finding: Non-canonical cell death pathways are implicated in neurodegenerative diseases, including Huntington's disease.
analysis.novel_insight: The review highlights the involvement of non-canonical cell death pathways in neurodegenerative diseases, providing a comprehensive overview of their molecular mechanisms.

41672134.title: P2X7 receptors as targets for neuroprotection.
41672134.abstract: In this review we explore the potential of P2X7 receptor blockers to elicit neuroprotection. This conjecture is based on a reasonably well-established role of this receptor in activating glial cells to maintain a chronic low-level neuroinflammatory state in the brain of patients suffering some neurodegenerative diseases (NDDs). In this context we briefly discuss evidence supporting the role of P2X7 receptors (P2X7) in the pathogenesis of Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, amyotrophic late

analysis.finding: P2X7 receptor blockers may elicit neuroprotection in various neurodegenerative diseases.

41693955.title: Dietary and metabolic reprogramming alleviates neurodegeneration: a review of mechanisms and clinical implications.
41693955.abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases, characterized by their insidious onset and progressive neuronal degeneration, present significant challenges in the fields of neuroscience and medicine. We elucidate the critical role of nutrition and cellular metabolism in the pathogenesis and progression of these disorders, with a particular focus on Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD), and Huntington's disease (HD). We demonstrate that fundamental nutrients such as glucose, lipids, and amino acids ar
41693955.authors: Fang L, Zhuang Y, Zhang M, Yang D, Zhang R et al. (7 authors)

analysis.finding: Nutrition and cellular metabolism play a critical role in the pathogenesis and progression of neurodegenerative diseases.

41636944.title: Phloretin as a Multitarget Neuroprotective Agent: Mechanistic Insights into the Modulation of Oxidative Stress, Inflammation, and Apoptosis.
41636944.abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases impose a substantial and growing global burden, affecting millions worldwide and leading to high medical, social, and economic costs. These are characterized by progressive neuronal dysfunction and loss, leading to cognitive, motor, and behavioral impairments. Neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, and Huntington's disease are driven by intertwined mechanisms of oxidative stress, neuroinflammation, protein aggregation, and neuronal

analysis.finding: Phloretin has multitarget neuroprotective effects by modulating oxidative stress, inflammation, and apoptosis.
analysis.novel_insight: Phloretin's ability to target multiple pathways involved in neurodegenerative diseases

41787540.title: How to improve statistical power in a trial with SCA2 patients using natural history data.
41787540.abstract: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trials are the gold standard for evaluating treatment effects, as they minimize biases. However, they raise ethical concerns regarding participant exposure to potentially ineffective interventions. For rare neurodegenerative diseases, these trials are further challenged by the limited number of patients. Our goal is to leverage recent advancements in statistical methodologies to enhance the statistical power of these trials. We aim to test these metho
41787540.authors: Tran M, Poulet PE, Petit E, Durr A, Klockgether T et al. (8 authors)

analysis.finding: Researchers developed a statistical model to improve the power of clinical trials for rare neurodegenerative diseases like SCA2 using natural history data.
analysis.novel_insight: The study proposes leveraging recent advancements in statistical methodologies to enhance trial efficiency, rather than introducing new treatments.

41933422.title: Sex-related differences in Huntington's disease: a scoping review.
41933422.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is an autosomal-dominant neurodegenerative disorder and emerging evidence indicates sex-specific differences in disease manifestation and progression. To summarize current evidence on sex-related differences in HD across genetic, clinical, biomarker, and treatment domains and identify gaps in knowledge. A literature search was performed in PubMed and Google Scholar (up to 31.01.2026). Eligible studies examined sex- or gender-related differences in individuals with genet
41933422.authors: Hemicker G, Schwarzová K, Cerejo C, Labrecque S, Peball M et al. (10 authors)

analysis.finding: The review aims to summarize current evidence regarding sex-specific differences in the manifestation and progression of Huntington's disease across genetic, clinical, biomarker, and treatment domains.
analysis.novel_insight: The systematic scoping review specifically focuses on identifying sex-related differences in HD, which is a key area of emerging research.

40874597.title: Therapeutic strategies for Huntington's disease: current approaches and future direction.
40874597.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is an autosomal, progressive, dominant inherited neurological disorder characterized by motor dysfunction, cognitive decline, and psychiatric symptoms. HD is caused by abnormal expansion of trinucleotide CAG in exon1 of the
40874597.authors: Gulzar M, Kauser S, Khan S, Adnan M, Hassan MI

analysis.finding: The paper reviews current and future therapeutic strategies for Huntington's disease, which is caused by an abnormal CAG trinucleotide expansion.
analysis.novel_insight: null

41537736.title: Rab4 spatially and functionally converges with Rab7 in the degradative endolysosomal network.
41537736.abstract: Rab GTPases are key regulators of endosomal trafficking in eukaryotes. In mammalian cells, Rab4 and Rab7 were shown to localize to distinct compartments, with Rab4 on early endosomes for fast recycling and Rab7 on late endosomes for degradation. Here, we show that in
41537736.authors: Farmer SM, Xu S, Yu Y, Ye X, Yang H et al. (19 authors)

analysis.finding: The study investigates the spatial and functional convergence of Rab4 and Rab7 within the degradative endolysosomal network.
analysis.novel_insight: The paper suggests a functional convergence between Rab4 (typically associated with recycling) and Rab7 (associated with degradation) in the endolysosomal network.

40517187.title: Crossing the blood-brain barrier: nanoparticle-based strategies for neurodegenerative disease therapy.
40517187.abstract: Neurodegenerative conditions, including Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and Huntington's disease, represent a critical medical challenge due to their increasing prevalence, severe consequences, and absence of curative treatments. Beyond the need for a deeper understanding of the fundamental mechanisms underlying neurodegeneration, the development of effective treatments is hindered by the blood-brain barrier, which poses a major obstacle to delivering therapeutic agents
40517187.authors: Haro-Martínez E, Muscolino E, Moral N, Duran J, Fornaguera C

analysis.finding: Nanoparticle-based strategies are being reviewed as a method to overcome the blood-brain barrier for treating various neurodegenerative diseases.
analysis.novel_insight: The review focuses on the general application of nanoparticles to overcome the BBB rather than a specific novel mechanism or compound.

40313114.title: Inherent potential of mitochondria-targeted interventions for chronic neurodegenerative diseases.
40313114.abstract: The cure rate for chronic neurodegenerative diseases remains low, creating an urgent need for improved intervention methods. Recent studies have shown that enhancing mitochondrial function can mitigate the effects of these diseases. This paper comprehensively reviews the relationship between mitochondrial dysfunction and chronic neurodegenerative diseases, aiming to uncover the potential use of targeted mitochondrial interventions as viable therapeutic options. We detail five targeted mitochondr
40313114.authors: Zhou M, Zheng M, Liang S, Li M, Ma J et al. (11 authors)

analysis.finding: Targeted mitochondrial interventions represent a promising area for treating chronic neurodegenerative diseases by addressing underlying mitochondrial dysfunction.
analysis.novel_insight: The paper comprehensively reviews five distinct, actionable strategies (mitophagy promotion, fission inhibition, biogenesis enhancement, antioxidant application, and mitochondrial transplantation) for intervention.

40536996.title: Neuroglobin: A promising candidate to treat neurological diseases.
40536996.abstract: Neurodevelopmental and neurodegenerative illnesses constitute a global health issue and a foremost economic burden since they are a large cause of incapacity and death worldwide. Altogether, the burden of neurological disorders has increased considerably over the past 30 years because of population aging. Overall, neurological diseases significantly impair cognitive and motor functions and their incidence will increase as societies age and the world's population continues to grow. Autism spectru
40536996.authors: Yañez IM, Torres-Cuevas I, Corral-Debrinski M

analysis.finding: The paper discusses the general burden of neurological disorders, listing Huntington's disease among many conditions caused by cellular homeostasis perturbations.
analysis.novel_insight: null

41926793.title: Extensive transcriptomic changes in cellular and animal models of Huntington's disease depending on the length of CAG repeats in the exon 1 of the HTT gene.
41926793.abstract: Although Huntington's disease - a severe, inherited, neurodegenerative disorder - is primarily caused by a pathological variant of the HTT gene (encoding huntingtin protein) which is characterized by the extension of CAG repeats in the 1st exon exceeding 36 triplets, the biochemical mechanisms underlying the disease remain poorly understood. Mutant huntingtin forms toxic aggregates in cells; however, the clinical symptoms usually appear in adulthood. Recent reports suggested that somatic expansi
41926793.authors: Szulc A, Walter BM, Gaffke L, Wiśniewska K, Żabińska M et al. (12 authors)

analysis.finding: The transcriptomic changes in cellular and animal models of Huntington's disease are dependent on the length of the CAG repeats in the HTT gene.
analysis.novel_insight: The study highlights the role of CAG repeat length in determining the transcriptomic profile, suggesting a dose-dependent mechanism for pathogenicity.

41925063.title: Combination Disease-Modifying Therapy for Neurodegenerative Diseases Using Repurposed Drugs.
41925063.abstract: We review the positive effects of several existing drugs from different classes, such as chemical chaperones, glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1 RAs), iron chelators, and cluster-Abelson tyrosine kinase inhibitors (c-Abl TKIs), in preclinical disease models and in available published human data following use of these drugs in individuals with common neurodegenerative diseases (NDs), including Parkinson's disease (PD), Alzheimer's disease (AD), amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), a

targets: multiple pathogenic processes involved in neurodegeneration
analysis.finding: The paper reviews the positive effects of various existing drugs from different classes for treating multiple neurodegenerative diseases, including Huntington's disease.
analysis.novel_insight: The paper proposes a strategy to design an ideal combination of drugs to address multiple pathogenic processes in neurodegeneration.

41591821.title: From Cancer to Neuroprotection: Pazopanib Modulates the RIPK1/RIPK3/MLKL and PGAM5/DRP1 Pathways in 3- Nitropropionic Acid-Induced Huntington's Disease.
41591821.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder with a poorly understood neurobiological basis. Among the various forms of programmed cell death implicated in neurodegeneration, necroptosis has recently garnered significant attention. Notably, dysregulated necroptosis provokes immune response that drives excessive pro-inflammatory cytokine production, promoting further necroptotic cell death through TNF-α/RIPK1 signaling. Consequently, necroptosis exacerbates neuroinflammat
41591821.authors: Eltarzy MA, Rabie MA, Sayed RH, Ahmed MAE, Ahmedy OA

analysis.finding: Pazopanib modulates the RIPK1/RIPK3/MLKL and PGAM5/DRP1 pathways, suggesting a role in mitigating necroptosis and mitochondrial dysfunction in HD.
analysis.novel_insight: The study links an anti-cancer agent (Pazopanib) to modulating specific necroptotic and mitochondrial pathways (RIPK1/RIPK3/MLKL and PGAM5/DRP1) in the context of HD pathogenesis.

41951733.title: Population-scale repeat expansions elucidate disease risk and brain atrophy.
41951733.abstract: Pathogenic expansions of short tandem repeats (STRs) cause over 70 neurological diseases
41951733.authors: Pounraja VK, Sul JH, Herman J, O'Keeffe S, Rajagopal V et al. (35 authors)

analysis.finding: The study analyzes population-scale repeat expansions to elucidate disease risk and associated brain atrophy in Huntington's disease.
analysis.novel_insight: Focus on population-scale analysis linking repeat expansions directly to measurable brain atrophy.

41944829.title: Quantifying brain-age gap in people with HIV on antiretroviral therapy using plasma neurofilament light chain.
41944829.abstract: To estimate the number of additional years above chronological age ("brain-age gap") at which observed plasma neurofilament light chain (NfL) levels in cognitively unimpaired people with HIV (PWH) correspond to published age-stratified standards from cognitively unimpaired individuals in the general population. Secondary analysis of the multicenter observational cohort study (ACTG HAILO) involving older, cognitively unimpaired PWH on antiretroviral therapy (ART). Plasma NfL concentrations were q
41944829.authors: Park H, Hyle EP, Tassiopoulos KK, Gisslén M, Rubin LH et al. (7 authors)

analysis.finding: Plasma NfL levels in cognitively unimpaired people with HIV can be used to estimate a 'brain-age gap' by comparing them to age-stratified general population standards.
analysis.novel_insight: The application of NfL to quantify brain-age gap specifically in the context of HIV infection and ART treatment.

41930586.title: AI-Driven Biomarker Discovery in Motor-Related Neurodegenerative Diseases.
41930586.abstract: Parkinson's disease (PD), Huntington's disease (HD), amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs) are examples of neurodegenerative disorders (NDDs) that share overlapping neuropathological processes and largely affect motor coordination. For early diagnosis, illness monitoring, and treatment targeting, it is essential to find trustworthy biomarkers that represent motor circuit dysfunction. The purpose of this study is to summarize the state of the art regarding molecu
41930586.authors: Pavithra N, Begum RF, Ashwini ST, Nirenjen S, Singh A et al. (8 authors)

analysis.finding: The study reviews current molecular, neurochemical, and imaging biomarkers for motor impairment in NDDs and explores the application of AI/ML for their discovery and validation.
analysis.novel_insight: The systematic investigation of AI/ML applications across multiple motor-related NDDs (PD, HD, ALS, SCAs) for biomarker discovery.

41915164.title: Spontaneous speech and language measures as predictive biomarkers of clinically meaningful disease progression and neurodegeneration in Huntington's disease.
41915164.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is characterized by heterogeneous rates of clinical progression, complicating patient monitoring and clinical trial design. Although speech and language alterations are increasingly recognized as part of the HD cognitive phenotype, their value as short-term prognostic biomarkers of clinically meaningful disease progression and neurodegeneration remains unestablished. In this prospective 12-month longitudinal study, we investigated whether objectively quantified spontane
41915164.authors: Puig-Davi A, Franch-Marti C, Caler-Gameiro L, Perez-Perez J, Olmedo-Saura G et al. (17 authors)

analysis.finding: Objectively quantified spontaneous speech and language measures were investigated as potential short-term prognostic biomarkers for clinically meaningful disease progression and neurodegeneration in Huntington's disease.
analysis.novel_insight: The study focuses on establishing the predictive value of *spontaneous* speech and language measures, rather than just standardized testing, for tracking short-term progression.

41951144.title: Longitudinal and combined assessment of 24(S)-hydroxycholesterol and Neurofilament light chain in the early stages of Huntington's disease.
41951144.abstract: Huntington's Disease (HD) is an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder. Recent clinical research has focused on neurofilament light chain protein (NfL), and cholesterol metabolites as potential fluid biomarkers related to disease progression. Our aim was to explore whether the combined longitudinal assessment of NfL and cholesterol-derived metabolites would improve the characterization of early HD stages. We enrolled 96 HD individuals and 63 healthy controls. Disease stage of HD participa
41951144.authors: Sarro L, Valenza M, Mongelli A, Passoni A, Castaldo A et al. (20 authors)

analysis.finding: The study aimed to determine if combining longitudinal assessments of NfL and cholesterol metabolites could improve the characterization of early Huntington's disease stages.
analysis.novel_insight: The combination of NfL and cholesterol metabolites for longitudinal assessment in early HD stages.

41918200.title: Neuroinflammation, Autophagy, and Neurodegeneration: Mechanisms and Therapeutic Insights.
41918200.abstract: Neuroinflammation and autophagy dysregulation are critical in the pathogenesis of neurodegenerative diseases like Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, and Huntington's disease. Neuroinflammation occurs after a sustained immune response, which transitions into a chronic pathological state, leading to the sustained generation of pro-inflammatory cytokines and oxidative stress, causing neuronal damage. Meanwhile, defective autophagy exacerbates disease by promoting protein accumulation, e.g., amyloid-β, tau,

analysis.finding: Neuroinflammation and defective autophagy create a vicious cycle that drives neuronal damage in Huntington's disease.
analysis.novel_insight: The paper synthesizes the interplay between mTOR/AMPK regulation, inflammation, and autophagy specifically in the context of HD pathogenesis.

41919473.title: Long non-coding RNAs in neurodegenerative diseases - Molecular mechanisms, liquid biopsy biomarkers, and therapeutic targets: A review.
41919473.abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases (NDDs), such as Alzheimer's disease (AD), Parkinson's disease (PD), amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), and Huntington's disease (HD), are age-related disorders characterized by progressive neuronal loss, cognitive decline, and limited options for disease-modifying treatments. Increasing evidence suggests that long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) play significant roles in neurodevelopment, neuronal homeostasis, and disease progression; however, their involvement in shared p
41919473.authors: Cheng Y, Qiu M, Yu Z, Tang X, Zhang J

analysis.finding: lncRNAs are implicated in the pathogenesis of multiple neurodegenerative diseases, including Huntington's disease, by modulating common disease mechanisms.
analysis.novel_insight: The review consolidates findings across multiple methodologies (experimental, transcriptomic, bioinformatic) to define the role of lncRNAs in shared pathogenic pathways of NDDs.

41287980.title: Quantitative relationship between tominersen concentrations in cerebrospinal fluid and biomarker changes in Huntington's disease patients.
41287980.abstract: Intrathecally administered antisense oligonucleotide tominersen aims to slow Huntington's disease progression by lowering mutant huntingtin (mHTT) protein levels. This study used non-linear mixed effects population pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic (PKPD) modelling to characterize the relationship between tominersen concentration in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) and CSF mHTT reduction. Additionally, the relationship between tominersen CSF exposure and changes in other CSF biomarkers was investigat
41287980.authors: Yamamoto Y, Silber Baumann HE, Björnsson M, Grimsey P, Hawellek DJ et al. (12 authors)

analysis.finding: The study characterized the relationship between tominersen concentration in cerebrospinal fluid and the reduction of CSF mHTT using non-linear mixed effects PKPD modeling.
analysis.novel_insight: Application of non-linear mixed effects PKPD modeling to precisely link CSF drug exposure to biomarker changes for dose selection in a major ongoing trial.

40145977.title: Potential targets of microglia in the treatment of neurodegenerative diseases: Mechanism and therapeutic implications.
40145977.abstract: For diverse neurodegenerative disorders, microglial cells are activated. Furthermore, dysfunctional and hyperactivated microglia initiate mitochondrial autophagy, oxidative stress, and pathological protein accumulation, ending with neuroinflammation that exacerbates damage to dopaminergic neurons and contributes significantly to the pathology of neurodegenerative disorder. Microglial over-activation is closely associated with the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines, the phagocytosis of injur
40145977.authors: Zhao W, Liu Z, Wu J, Liu A, Yan J

analysis.finding: Microglial dysfunction, characterized by hyperactivation, contributes significantly to neurodegeneration through oxidative stress, mitochondrial autophagy, and neuroinflammation.
analysis.novel_insight: The review summarizes the general role of microglia across multiple neurodegenerative diseases rather than focusing on a single novel mechanism or target.

41885638.title: Cryptoxanthin as a multitarget neuroprotective agent: mechanistic and in silico perspectives.
41885638.abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases such as Parkinson's disease (PD), Huntington's disease (HD), Alzheimer's disease (AD), and Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) are complex disorders driven by multiple pathological processes, including oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, protein misfolding, and neuroinflammation. Due to this multifactorial nature, there is a growing interest in identifying natural compounds with multi-targeted neuroprotective properties. This review aims to evaluate the therap
41885638.journal: The Journal of pharmacy and pharmacology
41885638.authors: Sadhukhan A, Chauhan A, Kumar M, Singh TG, Mujwar S et al. (6 authors)

analysis.finding: β-cryptoxanthin shows potential as a multi-targeted neuroprotective agent for various neurodegenerative diseases.
analysis.novel_insight: The review focuses on evaluating the broad, multi-targeted neuroprotective potential of a natural carotenoid, β-cryptoxanthin, rather than a specific mechanism or genetic pathway.

42035261.title: Treatment of Huntington's disease with a pan-HTT-targeting CRISPR nuclease.
42035261.abstract: Huntington's disease (HD) is an inherited neurodegenerative disorder caused by an expansion of a CAG trinucleotide repeat in the huntingtin (HTT) gene, which leads to a mutant protein that destroys neurons in the brain. Despite intense effort, there remains no approved disease-modifying therapy for HD. Here we develop a pan-HTT-targeting CRISPR-Cas9 system that, when delivered to the striatum of R6/2 and YAC128 mice by AAV5, lowered mutant HTT mRNA and protein by 55-80% via its induction of fram
42035261.journal: Molecular therapy : the journal of the American Society of Gene Therapy
42035261.authors: Tan K, Del Bosque Siller D, Xiong AY, Wang AXA, McCallister TX et al. (16 authors)

analysis.finding: A pan-HTT-targeting CRISPR-Cas9 system successfully reduced mutant HTT mRNA and protein levels by 55-80% and significantly improved motor and behavioral phenotypes in mouse models of HD.
analysis.novel_insight: The successful development of a pan-HTT-targeting CRISPR system that achieves significant molecular knockdown and functional behavioral rescue in vivo.

41552385.title: An acyclic nucleic acid-modified siRNA targeting CAG expansions for polyglutamine disease treatment.
41552385.abstract: Polyglutamine (polyQ) diseases are inherited neurological disorders caused by an expansion of the cytosine-adenine-guanine (CAG) repeat in the causative genes. These include Huntington's disease, spinal and bulbar muscular atrophy (SBMA), and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Clinical trials have been conducted using nucleic acid therapeutics to silence the causative gene for these diseases, but none have been approved for use. Furthermore, while oligonucleotides targeting the CAG repeats are an a
41552385.authors: Maeda K, Hirunagi T, Sahashi K, Kamiya Y, Iida M et al. (12 authors)

analysis.finding: The study developed an acyclic serinol nucleic acid (SNA)-modified siRNA designed to specifically target pathogenic CAG repeats for polyglutamine disease treatment.
analysis.novel_insight: The use of SNA modification in the siRNA structure is novel, aiming to improve specificity and prevent concomitant silencing of the wild-type allele.

42041587.title: Gene Editing Strategies for Neurological and Mental Disorders: Advances in Delivery, Methodology, and Clinical Translation.
42041587.abstract: Neurological and mental disorders are among the main causes of disability worldwide, affecting over three billion people and increasing the socioeconomic burden. Advances in molecular genetics and genome engineering have led to gene-targeted therapies that address root causes rather than just symptoms. This review covers current genome-editing tools, including CRISPR/Cas, base editing, and prime editing. The focus is on the benefits of gene editing in the central nervous system, where post-mitot

analysis.finding: Gene editing tools offer a lasting, root-cause therapeutic approach for CNS disorders, contingent upon effective delivery across the blood-brain barrier.
analysis.novel_insight: The integration of advanced editing techniques (prime/base editing) with non-viral delivery systems (nanoparticles/exosomes) to overcome the blood-brain barrier represents a major translational advance.

42037491.title: Iloperidone treatment mitigates the Juvenile Huntington's Disease phenotype possibly via Sigma-1 Receptor Modulation.
42037491.abstract: Juvenile Huntington's disease (jHD) is a severe genetic disorder with combined neurodegenerative and neurodevelopmental features, characterized by early onset and markedly reduced life expectancy. Despite extensive efforts, drug development has been largely unsuccessful and no effective therapy capable of mitigating the pathological phenotype is currently available. Here, we investigated the potential of the FDA-approved drug iloperidone to ameliorate disease-related phenotypes in cortical neuro
42037491.authors: Fornetti E, Galluzzi G, Frezzini M, Exertier C, Brufani V et al. (12 authors)

analysis.finding: Iloperidone treatment showed potential to ameliorate juvenile Huntington's disease phenotypes in patient-derived cortical neurons, possibly through Sigma-1 Receptor modulation.
analysis.novel_insight: The investigation proposes that the therapeutic effect of the existing drug iloperidone in jHD is mediated by activating S1R-dependent proteostasis pathways.

42007924.title: Towards AI-driven prediction of HTT CAG size in super-expanded human spiny projection neurons from Huntington disease donors.
42007924.abstract: Somatic instability (SI) of the CAG tract in HTT is a major driver of neurodegeneration of Spiny Projection Neurons (SPNs), the primary neuronal subtype affected in Huntington's disease (HD). SPNs can accumulate hundreds of CAG repeats during a patient's lifetime, and once the expansion exceeds ∼150 CAGs, they acquire distinct, cell-autonomous transcriptional alterations that ultimately contribute to degeneration. Here, we developed the "HD-Phase-Model", a mathematical framework designed to iden
42007924.authors: Maestri S, Scalzo D, Zobel M, Besusso D, Cattaneo E

analysis.finding: A mathematical framework, the HD-Phase-Model, was developed to identify super-expanded Spiny Projection Neurons based solely on their transcriptional profile, bypassing the need for direct CAG repeat sizing.
analysis.novel_insight: The model successfully links the pathological state of super-expansion (a physical characteristic) to distinct transcriptional alterations using only single-nucleus transcriptomic data, which is a significant advancement over methods requiring direct repeat sizing.
